frontmatter.title: Optimality Conditions for Elliptic Optimal Control with Constraints

children.value:
The previous lecture established the continuous framework for linear-quadratic elliptic
control:

children.html: min⁡u∈Qf(u),\min_{u\in Q} f(u),u∈Qmin​f(u),
V×Q×V,V:=H01(Ω),Q:=L2(Ω),V\times Q\times V, \qquad V:=H_0^1(\Omega),\quad Q:=L^2(\Omega),V×Q×V,V:=H01​(Ω),Q:=L2(Ω),
∇f(u)=p+αu,\nabla f(u)=p+\alpha u,∇f(u)=p+αu,

children.value: and the unconstrained KKT system in dual spaces.

This lecture extends the framework to the case of
control constraints.
the variational inequality for box-constrained controls;
the operatorial KKT system with a nonlinear control condition;
the saddle-point interpretation in infinite dimensions.

children.html: V:=H01(Ω),Q:=L2(Ω).V:=H_0^1(\Omega), \qquad Q:=L^2(\Omega).V:=H01​(Ω),Q:=L2(Ω).

children.value: From the previous lecture, in the unconstrained case, the first-order system is

children.html: Ay−Bu=Fin V′,My−A∗p=Mydin V′,αRQu+B∗p=0in Q′.\begin{aligned} A y - B u &= F &&\text{in }V',\\ M y - A^* p &= M y_d &&\text{in }V',\\ \alpha R_Q u + B^* p &= 0 &&\text{in }Q'. \end{aligned}Ay−BuMy−A∗pαRQ​u+B∗p​=F=Myd​=0​​in V′,in V′,in Q′.​
∇f(u)=p+αu.\nabla f(u)=p+\alpha u.∇f(u)=p+αu.

children.value: This is the only fact we need to import from the previous lecture.
We now impose box constraints on the control:

U_{\mathrm{ad}}
:=
\left\{
u\in Q:
u_{\min}(x)\le u(x)\le u_{\max}(x)
\text{ for a.e. }x\in\Omega
\right\}.
children.html: Uad:={u∈Q:umin⁡(x)≤u(x)≤umax⁡(x) for a.e. x∈Ω}.U_{\mathrm{ad}} := \left\{ u\in Q: u_{\min}(x)\le u(x)\le u_{\max}(x) \text{ for a.e. }x\in\Omega \right\}.Uad​:={u∈Q:umin​(x)≤u(x)≤umax​(x) for a.e. x∈Ω}.
umin⁡,umax⁡∈L∞(Ω),umin⁡(x)≤umax⁡(x)a.e. in Ω,u_{\min},u_{\max}\in L^\infty(\Omega), \qquad u_{\min}(x)\le u_{\max}(x) \quad \text{a.e. in }\Omega,umin​,umax​∈L∞(Ω),umin​(x)≤umax​(x)a.e. in Ω,
Uad⊂QU_{\mathrm{ad}}\subset QUad​⊂Q

children.value: The constrained reduced problem is therefore

children.html: min⁡u∈Uadf(u).\min_{u\in U_{\mathrm{ad}}} f(u).u∈Uad​min​f(u).

children.value:
The state equation is unchanged.
Only the admissible set for the control changes.

For minimization of a differentiable functional over a closed convex set in a Hilbert
space, the first-order condition is a variational inequality rather than the equation

children.html: ∇f(uˉ)=0\nabla f(\bar u)=0∇f(uˉ)=0
uˉ∈Uad\bar u\in U_{\mathrm{ad}}uˉ∈Uad​

children.value: is optimal if and only if

children.html: f′(uˉ)(u−uˉ)≥0∀u∈Uad.f'(\bar u)(u-\bar u)\ge 0 \qquad \forall u\in U_{\mathrm{ad}}.f′(uˉ)(u−uˉ)≥0∀u∈Uad​.

children.value: Using the reduced gradient from the previous lecture, this becomes

children.html: (pˉ+αuˉ,u−uˉ)Q≥0∀u∈Uad.(\bar p+\alpha \bar u,u-\bar u)_Q\ge 0 \qquad \forall u\in U_{\mathrm{ad}}.(pˉ​+αuˉ,u−uˉ)Q​≥0∀u∈Uad​.

children.value:
This is the constrained analogue of stationarity.
If the constraint is inactive, one recovers

children.html: pˉ+αuˉ=0\bar p+\alpha \bar u=0pˉ​+αuˉ=0

children.value:
.
If a bound is active, only admissible perturbations are allowed, so equality is replaced
by an inequality.

The cleanest way to express the constrained problem in the operatorial setting is through
the normal cone to

children.html: UadU_{\mathrm{ad}}Uad​
K⊂QK\subset QK⊂Q
u∈Ku\in Ku∈K
NK(u):={η∈Q′:⟨η,v−u⟩Q′,Q≤0∀v∈K}.N_K(u) := \left\{ \eta\in Q': \langle \eta,v-u\rangle_{Q',Q}\le 0 \quad \forall v\in K \right\}.NK​(u):={η∈Q′:⟨η,v−u⟩Q′,Q​≤0∀v∈K}.

children.value: Then the variational inequality is equivalent to the inclusion

children.html: 0∈αRQuˉ+B∗pˉ+NUad(uˉ)in Q′.0\in \alpha R_Q \bar u+B^*\bar p+N_{U_{\mathrm{ad}}}(\bar u) \qquad \text{in }Q'.0∈αRQ​uˉ+B∗pˉ​+NUad​​(uˉ)in Q′.

children.value: Therefore the full KKT system becomes

children.value:
Compared with the unconstrained system from the previous lecture, only the third equation changes:
the linear equation in

children.value:
So the constrained problem still has the same KKT block structure as in the previous lecture,
but now one block is nonlinear because of the cone term.

For box constraints, the normal-cone inclusion has a pointwise characterization.
It is equivalent to the projection formula

children.html: uˉ=Π[umin⁡,umax⁡](−1αpˉ),\bar u = \Pi_{[u_{\min},u_{\max}]} \left(-\frac1\alpha \bar p\right),uˉ=Π[umin​,umax​]​(−α1​pˉ​),
Π[umin⁡,umax⁡]\Pi_{[u_{\min},u_{\max}]}Π[umin​,umax​]​

children.value: denotes the pointwise projection onto the interval

children.html: [umin⁡(x),umax⁡(x)][u_{\min}(x),u_{\max}(x)][umin​(x),umax​(x)]
x∈Ωx\in\Omegax∈Ω

\bar u(x)=
\begin{cases}
u_{\min}(x)
& \text{if } -\dfrac1\alpha \bar p(x)\le u_{\min}(x),\\[6pt]
u_{\max}(x)
& \text{if } -\dfrac1\alpha \bar p(x)\ge u_{\max}(x),\\[6pt]
-\dfrac1\alpha \bar p(x)
& \text{if } u_{\min}(x)<-\dfrac1\alpha \bar p(x)<u_{\max}(x).
\end{cases}
children.html: uˉ(x)={umin⁡(x)if −1αpˉ(x)≤umin⁡(x),umax⁡(x)if −1αpˉ(x)≥umax⁡(x),−1αpˉ(x)if umin⁡(x)<−1αpˉ(x)<umax⁡(x).\bar u(x)= \begin{cases} u_{\min}(x) & \text{if } -\dfrac1\alpha \bar p(x)\le u_{\min}(x),\\[6pt] u_{\max}(x) & \text{if } -\dfrac1\alpha \bar p(x)\ge u_{\max}(x),\\[6pt] -\dfrac1\alpha \bar p(x) & \text{if } u_{\min}(x)<-\dfrac1\alpha \bar p(x)<u_{\max}(x). \end{cases}uˉ(x)=⎩⎨⎧​umin​(x)umax​(x)−α1​pˉ​(x)​if −α1​pˉ​(x)≤umin​(x),if −α1​pˉ​(x)≥umax​(x),if umin​(x)<−α1​pˉ​(x)<umax​(x).​

children.value:
Thus the control law remains explicit, but it is no longer linear.
The unconstrained formula

children.html: u=−α−1pu=-\alpha^{-1}pu=−α−1p

children.value: is simply clipped onto the admissible interval.
Combining state equation, adjoint equation, and control characterization, we obtain

children.html: {Ayˉ−Buˉ=Fin V′,Myˉ−A∗pˉ=Mydin V′,uˉ=ΠUad ⁣(−1αpˉ)in Q.\begin{cases} A \bar y - B \bar u = F & \text{in }V',\\[4pt] M \bar y - A^* \bar p = M y_d & \text{in }V',\\[4pt] \bar u = \Pi_{U_{\mathrm{ad}}}\!\left(-\dfrac1\alpha \bar p\right) & \text{in }Q. \end{cases}⎩⎨⎧​Ayˉ​−Buˉ=FMyˉ​−A∗pˉ​=Myd​uˉ=ΠUad​​(−α1​pˉ​)​in V′,in V′,in Q.​

children.value: In the Poisson model of this course, this reads

children.html: {−Δyˉ=uˉ+fin Ω,−Δpˉ=yˉ−ydin Ω,uˉ=ΠUad ⁣(−1αpˉ)in Ω,yˉ=pˉ=0on ∂Ω.\begin{cases} -\Delta \bar y = \bar u + f & \text{in }\Omega,\\[4pt] -\Delta \bar p = \bar y-y_d & \text{in }\Omega,\\[4pt] \bar u = \Pi_{U_{\mathrm{ad}}}\!\left(-\dfrac1\alpha \bar p\right) & \text{in }\Omega,\\[4pt] \bar y=\bar p=0 & \text{on }\partial\Omega. \end{cases}⎩⎨⎧​−Δyˉ​=uˉ+f−Δpˉ​=yˉ​−yd​uˉ=ΠUad​​(−α1​pˉ​)yˉ​=pˉ​=0​in Ω,in Ω,in Ω,on ∂Ω.​

children.value:
The first two equations are linear and exactly the same kind of objects as in the previous lecture.
The third one carries the entire effect of the control constraint.
The constrained system is the direct analogue of finite-dimensional KKT conditions:

children.html: V×Q×VV\times Q\times VV×Q×V

children.value: the equations live in the dual product space

children.html: V′×Q′×V′V'\times Q'\times V'V′×Q′×V′

children.value: the state and adjoint equations are linear operator equations;

the control condition is a complementarity relation encoded either as a normal-cone inclusion
or as a projection formula.
current lecture: saddle-point system with a nonlinear control block.
This is exactly the structure later exploited by active-set and semismooth Newton methods.
The most direct numerical method works on the reduced functional over the convex set

children.html: UadU_{\mathrm{ad}}Uad​
u0∈Uadu^0\in U_{\mathrm{ad}}u0∈Uad​
Ayk−Buk=F;A y^k - B u^k = F;Ayk−Buk=F;

children.value: M y^k - A^* p^k = M y_d;
children.html: Myk−A∗pk=Myd;M y^k - A^* p^k = M y_d;Myk−A∗pk=Myd​;
gk=∇f(uk)=pk+αuk;g^k=\nabla f(u^k)=p^k+\alpha u^k;gk=∇f(uk)=pk+αuk;
uk+1=ΠUad ⁣(uk−τkgk).u^{k+1} = \Pi_{U_{\mathrm{ad}}}\!\left(u^k-\tau_k g^k\right).uk+1=ΠUad​​(uk−τk​gk).

children.value:
This is the Hilbert-space analogue of projected gradient descent for bound-constrained
finite-dimensional optimization.
the projection enforces admissibility at every iteration;
each step still requires one state solve and one adjoint solve, exactly as in the unconstrained reduced method.
For the linear-quadratic case, if one chooses

children.html: τk=1α,\tau_k=\frac1\alpha,τk​=α1​,
uk−τkgk=uk−1α(αuk+pk)=−1αpk,u^k-\tau_k g^k = u^k-\frac1\alpha(\alpha u^k+p^k) = -\frac1\alpha p^k,uk−τk​gk=uk−α1​(αuk+pk)=−α1​pk,
uk+1=ΠUad ⁣(−1αpk).u^{k+1} = \Pi_{U_{\mathrm{ad}}}\!\left(-\frac1\alpha p^k\right).uk+1=ΠUad​​(−α1​pk).

children.value:
This explains why the projection formula is both an optimality condition and a natural
fixed-point iteration.

Projected gradient is robust and easy to implement, but it is usually only linearly
convergent and may become slow when the active set is nearly identified but not yet fixed.

The projection formula suggests splitting the domain into active and inactive regions.
Given an iterate

children.html: (uk,pk)(u^k,p^k)(uk,pk)
A−k:={x∈Ω:−1αpk(x)≤umin⁡(x)},\mathcal A_-^k := \left\{ x\in\Omega: -\frac1\alpha p^k(x)\le u_{\min}(x) \right\},A−k​:={x∈Ω:−α1​pk(x)≤umin​(x)},
A+k:={x∈Ω:−1αpk(x)≥umax⁡(x)},\mathcal A_+^k := \left\{ x\in\Omega: -\frac1\alpha p^k(x)\ge u_{\max}(x) \right\},A+k​:={x∈Ω:−α1​pk(x)≥umax​(x)},
Ik:=Ω∖(A−k∪A+k).\mathcal I^k := \Omega\setminus(\mathcal A_-^k\cup \mathcal A_+^k).Ik:=Ω∖(A−k​∪A+k​).

children.value: To make the primal-dual structure explicit, introduce a multiplier

children.html: λ∈Q′,\lambda\in Q',λ∈Q′,

children.value: for the box constraints, and write the control optimality condition as

children.html: αRQu+B∗p+λ=0in Q′.\alpha R_Q u + B^* p + \lambda = 0 \qquad \text{in }Q'.αRQ​u+B∗p+λ=0in Q′.
λ\lambdaλ

children.value:
\lambda(x)\ge 0 \ \text{on the lower active set},
\qquad
\lambda(x)\le 0 \ \text{on the upper active set},
\qquad
\lambda(x)=0 \ \text{on the inactive set}.
children.html: λ(x)≥0 on the lower active set,λ(x)≤0 on the upper active set,λ(x)=0 on the inactive set.\lambda(x)\ge 0 \ \text{on the lower active set}, \qquad \lambda(x)\le 0 \ \text{on the upper active set}, \qquad \lambda(x)=0 \ \text{on the inactive set}.λ(x)≥0 on the lower active set,λ(x)≤0 on the upper active set,λ(x)=0 on the inactive set.

children.value:
Once the active sets are frozen, the inequalities are replaced by equality constraints.
On a given active-set guess

children.html: (A−k,A+k,Ik)(\mathcal A_-^k,\mathcal A_+^k,\mathcal I^k)(A−k​,A+k​,Ik)

children.value:
, one may therefore
consider the Lagrangian

children.html: Lk(y,u,p,λ−k,λ+k):=12∥y−yd∥L2(Ω)2+α2∥u∥L2(Ω)2−⟨Ay−Bu−F,p⟩V′,V+(λ−k,u−umin⁡)L2(A−k)+(λ+k,u−umax⁡)L2(A+k),\begin{aligned} \mathcal L_k(y,u,p,\lambda_-^k,\lambda_+^k) :={}& \frac12\|y-y_d\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 +\frac{\alpha}{2}\|u\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 \\ &-\langle Ay-Bu-F,p\rangle_{V',V}\\ &+(\lambda_-^k,u-u_{\min})_{L^2(\mathcal A_-^k)} +(\lambda_+^k,u-u_{\max})_{L^2(\mathcal A_+^k)}, \end{aligned}Lk​(y,u,p,λ−k​,λ+k​):=​21​∥y−yd​∥L2(Ω)2​+2α​∥u∥L2(Ω)2​−⟨Ay−Bu−F,p⟩V′,V​+(λ−k​,u−umin​)L2(A−k​)​+(λ+k​,u−umax​)L2(A+k​)​,​

children.value:
where the equality constraints are imposed only on the active sets.
The multipliers

αuk+1+pk+1=0on Ik.\alpha u^{k+1}+p^{k+1}=0 \qquad \text{on }\mathcal I^k.αuk+1+pk+1=0on Ik.

λk+1=0on Ik,\lambda^{k+1}=0 \qquad \text{on }\mathcal I^k,λk+1=0on Ik,
A−k\mathcal A_-^kA−k​

children.value: So, once the active sets are guessed, one solves a linear system for

children.html: (yk+1,uk+1,pk+1,λk+1)(y^{k+1},u^{k+1},p^{k+1},\lambda^{k+1})(yk+1,uk+1,pk+1,λk+1)

children.value:
with those sets frozen.
In strong form this means:

\begin{cases}
-\Delta y^{k+1}=u^{k+1}+f,\\[4pt]
-\Delta p^{k+1}=y^{k+1}-y_d,\\[4pt]
u^{k+1}=u_{\min}\ \text{on }\mathcal A_-^k,\\[4pt]
u^{k+1}=u_{\max}\ \text{on }\mathcal A_+^k,\\[4pt]
\alpha u^{k+1}+p^{k+1}=0\ \text{on }\mathcal I^k.
\end{cases}
children.html: {−Δyk+1=uk+1+f,−Δpk+1=yk+1−yd,uk+1=umin⁡ on A−k,uk+1=umax⁡ on A+k,αuk+1+pk+1=0 on Ik.\begin{cases} -\Delta y^{k+1}=u^{k+1}+f,\\[4pt] -\Delta p^{k+1}=y^{k+1}-y_d,\\[4pt] u^{k+1}=u_{\min}\ \text{on }\mathcal A_-^k,\\[4pt] u^{k+1}=u_{\max}\ \text{on }\mathcal A_+^k,\\[4pt] \alpha u^{k+1}+p^{k+1}=0\ \text{on }\mathcal I^k. \end{cases}⎩⎨⎧​−Δyk+1=uk+1+f,−Δpk+1=yk+1−yd​,uk+1=umin​ on A−k​,uk+1=umax​ on A+k​,αuk+1+pk+1=0 on Ik.​

children.value: guess active and inactive sets from the current iterate;
solve the equality-constrained KKT system with these sets fixed;
update the sets and repeat until they stop changing.
This is called a primal-dual active set method because:

children.html: uuu
ppp
λ\lambdaλ

children.value: decide whether a point should be active or inactive.

In practice, PDAS is often much faster than projected gradient because once the active set
is identified, the remaining step is essentially the solution of the correct linearized KKT system.

The same idea extends beyond box constraints.
Let

children.html: Uad⊂QU_{\mathrm{ad}}\subset QUad​⊂Q

children.value: be any closed convex set for which the projection

children.html: ΠUad:Q→Uad\Pi_{U_{\mathrm{ad}}}:Q\to U_{\mathrm{ad}}ΠUad​​:Q→Uad​

children.value:
is available in explicit or computational form.
Then one may still write the optimality condition as

children.html: u=ΠUad ⁣(u−σ∇f(u)),u=\Pi_{U_{\mathrm{ad}}}\!\left(u-\sigma \nabla f(u)\right),u=ΠUad​​(u−σ∇f(u)),

children.value: or equivalently as a normal-cone inclusion

children.html: 0∈∇f(u)+NUad(u),0\in \nabla f(u)+N_{U_{\mathrm{ad}}}(u),0∈∇f(u)+NUad​​(u),

children.value:
and build active-set type methods by locally identifying the part of the constraint
that behaves as an equality constraint at the current iterate.
For box constraints this identification is pointwise and especially transparent,
which is why PDAS takes its simplest form there.
For more general admissible sets, the same principle survives, but the geometry of
the active manifold and the structure of the projected Newton step become more problem-dependent.

The projection operator is not differentiable in the classical sense, but it is
semismooth.
This allows one to apply generalized Newton methods to the nonsmooth optimality system.

children.html: G:Q→QG:Q\to QG:Q→Q

children.value:
be locally Lipschitz and directionally differentiable.
Its generalized derivative at

children.html: uuu

∂G(u):=co⁡{lim⁡j→∞G′(uj):uj→u,  G differentiable at uj},\partial G(u) := \operatorname{co} \left\{ \lim_{j\to\infty} G'(u_j): u_j\to u,\; G \text{ differentiable at }u_j \right\},∂G(u):=co{j→∞lim​G′(uj​):uj​→u,G differentiable at uj​},

children.value:
that is, the convex hull of all limits of classical derivatives computed at nearby
points where

GGG
uuu

Semismoothness is the regularity that replaces classical differentiability in
generalized Newton theory.

For box constraints, the projection acts pointwise, so its generalized derivative is
easy to characterize:
on points that are projected onto an active bound;
Therefore a generalized Newton step for

children.html: G(u)=0G(u)=0G(u)=0

children.value:
amounts to freezing the current active set
and solving the corresponding linearized state-adjoint-control system.
This is exactly the mechanism behind PDAS.
So, in the linear-quadratic box-constrained case, one can view:
semismooth Newton as a generalized Newton method for the projection equation;
and, in this specific setting, the two viewpoints are essentially equivalent.

This equivalence is one of the main reasons why active-set methods are so effective for
elliptic control problems with box constraints: they inherit Newton-type local behavior
while preserving the simple geometric interpretation of active and inactive regions.
The three methods fit the same state-adjoint-control structure, but they use it differently:
projected gradient uses the projection only as a feasible descent step;
PDAS uses the projection to identify active sets and then solves a linear KKT system;
semismooth Newton interprets the projection equation as a nonsmooth root-finding problem.
projected gradient: cheapest iteration, strongest robustness, slowest asymptotically;
PDAS: more expensive iteration, often very fast once the active set is close to correct;
semismooth Newton: Newton-type local convergence, but requires a good formulation and linear solver strategy.

This is why the continuous optimality system matters algorithmically:
it tells us not only what the solution must satisfy, but also what class of numerical
methods is natural for the problem.
This lecture builds directly on the previous one:
